DEFENDANTS ESTEE LAUDER INC. AND THE ESTEE LAUDER COMPANIES INC.S NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Aug-20-2024 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (Part 3 of 3)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication of Punitive Damages is GRANTED. For a corporate defendant, the oppression, fraud or malice 'must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent of the corporation.' (Civ. Code, section 3294, subd. (b).) However, a finding of malice against a corporation is appropriate when the malicious behavior is 'corporate policy,' rather than the behavior of a rogue employee whose conduct was unknown to those with management authority. (Cruz v. Home Base (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 160, 167-68.) So long as the offending conduct is authorized by the corporation, that corporation may be liable for punitive damages without any direct communication with the injured party. (Bullock v. Philip Morris USA, Inc. (2008) 159 Cal.App.4th 655, 767-77.) Here, Plaintiff did not present a triable issue of material fact that Defendant acted with conscious disregard for its consumer's health and safety.

Prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order that tracks the tentative ruling verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: K. Septien. Matter Not Reported. Bailiff not present. (301/RCE)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Aug-20-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Rupal Shah of Frost Law Firm (866-353-6376), appearing for Plaintiff
Andrew Seitz of Frost Law Firm (866-353-6376), appearing for Plaintiff

The matter is not contested. The Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Estee Lauder, Inc's., Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant.

Defendant's evidentiary objections to Plaintiff's Exhibits 3, 4, 5, 7 ,8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 17, 18, and 19 in the Shah Declaration are sustained. Defendant's evidentiary objections to Plaintiff's Exhibits 20 and 21 (Affidavit of Mark Bailey, O, F, and Geology Report) are overruled.

Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) The relevant issue upon consideration of Defendant's Motion is whether Defendant has produced evidence proving that 'plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence' to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.)

Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's testimony precludes the possibility of Defendant having been a source of asbestos exposure because Plaintiff did not recall the brand name or product line of any blush she associates with Defendant is not persuasive. Plaintiff testified that she would buy Estee Lauder blush from I. Magnin, a store in Union Square of San Francisco. (P's Exh. 1 [P's Depo.] at 66:6-10.) Plaintiff recalled that she used a pinkish color of pressed powder blush. (Id. at 273:5-7.) Plaintiff testified that she was loyal to Defendant's brand from when she was wearing makeup during her employment at Roos Brothers in 1957. (Id. at 76:6-8.) The fact that Plaintiff did not recall the name and/or container of Defendant's products that she used does not create an inference of non-exposure or of the inability to prove exposure though other sources of evidence than Plaintiff's testimony. A plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) (Emphasis added.)

Additionally, Defendant argues in its Reply that Plaintiff cannot establish that she used a product from Defendant that contained talc. However, Defendant's PMQ testified that one of the products historically used by Defendant is talc. (P's Exh. 2 [Alferi Depo.] at 21:3-5.) She went on to testify that Talc 1621, also listed as Superfino Talc 1706 on ELC's batch production, was identified by its internal talc code 69-0154 known to be Italian talc. (P's Ex. 16 [Alferi Depo.] at. 237:16-239:12, 335:13-24.) Plaintiff argues that her geology expert Mark Bailey will testify that Italian talc has been shown to contain asbestos from the initial ore to the final product. (P's Ex. 20, [Bailey Affidavit] at p. 7-11; Ex. 21, [Bailey Report on Italian Talc, Oct. 30, 2020,] at p. 34-35.) The owners of the Italian mine were well-aware of the presence of asbestos in its mine, confirming the presence of 'Very hard silicates, as chrysotiles and tremolites, of amphibolitic derivation, namely with strict asbestos content.' (Id. at p. 33-34.) Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiff may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant.
(Minutes continued in next entry)

DEFENDANTS ESTEE LAUDER INC. AND THE ESTEE LAUDER COMPANIES INC.S NOTICE OF MOTION AND MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR ISSUANCE OF TENTATIVE RULING ONLY)
Off Calendar Aug-20-2024 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES: (Part 2 of 3)
(Mini Minutes continued from previous entry)

Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication is GRANTED, in part, and DENIED in part.

Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication is DENIED as to the Fourth Cause of Action- Intentional Tort/Intentional Failure to Warn/Concealment. Generally, nondisclosure or concealment may constitute actionable fraud when the defendant is in a fiduciary relationship with the plaintiff. (See, Bigler-Engler v. Breg, Inc. (2017) 7 Cal.App.5th 276, 311.) When a fiduciary duty does not exist, a duty to disclose arises only 'when the defendant had exclusive knowledge of material facts not known to the plaintiff,' or 'when the defendant actively conceals a material fact from the plaintiff,' or 'when the defendant makes partial representations but also suppresses some material facts.' (Id. at 311.) This type of relationship 'can only come into being as a result of some sort of transaction between the parties' and 'must necessarily arise from direct dealings between the plaintiff and the defendant; it cannot arise between the defendant and the public at large.' (Ibid.) Evidence that the defendant was involved in retail sales of the disputed product to consumers and profited from them can satisfy the Bigler requirement. (Bader v. Johnson & Johnson (2022) 86 Cal.App.5th 1094, 1132.)

Here, Defendant has only shown that Plaintiff had no direct contact with Defendant. Defendant has not shown through its evidence, however, that it was not involved in retail sales of the products and did not profit from those retail sales. On the contrary, the testimony shows that the Estee Lauder products were sold at I. Magnin (where Plaintiff purchased them). Accordingly, Defendant did not show that Plaintiff cannot reasonably obtain evidence of Defendant's involvement in retail sales of the products and profit from those sales, giving rise to a duty to disclose.

Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication is GRANTED as to the Second Cause of Action- False Misrepresentation. The elements of a cause of action for fraud are: (1) a misrepresentation, which includes a concealment or nondisclosure; (2) knowledge of the falsity of the misrepresentation, i.e., scienter; (3) intent to induce reliance on the misrepresentation; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damages. (Small v. Fritz Companies, Inc. (2003) 30 Cal.4th 167, 173.) Plaintiffs failed to put forth evidence of any specific misrepresentation of a material fact made by Defendant.
